findings.title: ~21% of all model births are mechanical: fertility logit degenerates to uniform (1/3,1/3,1/3) on populated infeasible nodes
findings.intended: Gumbel-smoothed fertility choice p_n ∝ exp(V_n/kappa_fert) over feasible options; infeasible states should carry no mass or no choice
findings.implemented: At nodes where all fertility options are the -1e10 sentinel, `lf = Vfa / P.kappa_fert; ls, pr = logsumexp(lf, axis=3)` returns pr = 1/3 for n=0,1,2 (verified numerically: 0.333333485667725 each), and the forward KFE applies these probabilities to the 20.4% of entrants sitting on infeasible nodes
findings.economic_consequence: 0.011986 artifact births at j=0 per cohort vs total cohort births ~0.0583: ~21% of all births come from uniform randomization among households that cannot afford the consumption floor. TFR net of the artifact ~1.57 vs reported 1.9832 (target 1.918); childless_rate, own_family_gap, fertility-income gradient, and mean age at first birth are contaminated in the direction that flatters the fit. The estimated fertility parameters (psi_child, kappa_fert, c_bar_n, h_bar_n) are fit against a moment vector ~1/5 of which is a numerical default.
findings.decisive_test: Zero out fert_probs rows where V(childless)<=-1e9 in a copy of the KFE (or drop invalid-node mass) and re-extract moments at the candidate theta: TFR should fall by ~0.4 and childless_rate rise sharply; run output/model/full_audit_20260711/scripts/audit_diag_locate.py to see the (1/3,1/3,1/3) rows directly
findings.evidence: solver.py:2309-2311 'lf = Vfa / P.kappa_fert; ls, pr = logsumexp(lf, axis=3); fert_probs[:, :, :, j, zz, :] = pr'. locate_invalid_j0.json: node b=-1.2326, z=0, n=0: V=-9999999997.92, fert_probs=[0.333333485667725, 0.333333485667725, 0.333333485667725], mass 3.6e-05; invalid j0 mass by parity exactly equal thirds [0.003995329733568752]*3.

findings.title: 20.4% of each entering cohort is injected onto Bellman-infeasible nodes; 1.72% of total population mass lives on V=-1e10 states following default policies
findings.intended: Entrants drawn from the PSID 25-35 wealth/income-ratio distribution should occupy feasible states, or infeasibility should be handled (mass shifted to nearest feasible node, or floors made affordable)
findings.implemented: entry_wealth_mode='income_ratio_distribution' spreads entrants over 27 grid nodes in [-2.907, +3.512]; at the candidate's fixed c_bar_0=1.28 the low-z/negative-wealth renter nodes have V=-1e10, yet 'g[int(kk), 0, i, 0, zz, 0, 0] += ...' injects mass there and the KFE evolves it with whatever policies the kernel left at infeasible nodes (c in [1.32,3.08], b' in [-1.10,2.74], tenure_choice in {0,1})
findings.economic_consequence: j=0 invalid populated mass 0.011986 = 20.4% of cohort (decaying to 0.0032 at j=1, 0.00095 at j=2); these households' consumption, saving, housing demand, and young-cohort moments (own_rate_2534, young liquid-wealth ratio, rooms moments) partly reflect undefined default policies rather than optimizing behavior; also poisons entry values (known gotcha)
findings.decisive_test: At the candidate theta, tabulate sum(g[V<=-1e9]) by age (already in diagnostics/current_bound/probe_checks.json 'populated_invalid'); compare young-cohort moments after reallocating entry mass to the nearest feasible node
findings.evidence: solver.py:3712-3716 'loc_entry_idx, loc_entry_wt = entry_wealth_grid_weights(...); g[int(kk), 0, i, 0, zz, 0, 0] += float(ww) * P.entry_by_loc[i] * z_weights[zz]'. calibration.py:51 '"entry_wealth_mode": "income_ratio_distribution"'. probe_checks.json: populated_invalid total 0.017195 (share 1.72%), by_age[0]=0.01199, wealth range [-2.907, 2.953], by_tenure renter 0.017115.

findings.title: 40% of childless fertile-window mass has birth probability exactly zero (birth option infeasible), so the extensive fertility margin is a hard feasibility corner, not a smoothed choice
findings.intended: kappa_fert=1.89 Gumbel smoothing implies interior birth probabilities that respond continuously to prices/wealth
findings.implemented: When V(n>=1) = -1e10 but V(n=0) is finite, the logit assigns p_birth = 0 exactly; this holds for 8.9% of cells but 40.3% of childless fertile-window mass (0.0980 of 0.2434), all on feasible nodes, mean wealth 0.0002 vs 0.1232 for all childless
findings.economic_consequence: Fertility comparative statics (the paper's housing-cost-to-fertility mechanism) operate through a discrete feasibility cliff for the poorest 40% of the childless; policy counterfactuals that relax housing costs will produce jumps as mass crosses the feasibility boundary, and kappa_fert is identified only off the remaining 60%
findings.decisive_test: Plot p_birth vs wealth at j=0..2 for z=0,1 (policy_childless_renter figures show the cliff); perturb p by ±1% at fixed theta and check for discontinuous jumps in TFR
findings.evidence: probe_checks.json p_birth_zero_decomposition: valid_node_mass=0.09798, invalid_node_mass=0.0, by_tenure renter 0.09185, by_age concentrated at j=0-2, mean_wealth_pbirth0=0.00018 vs 0.12324; numeric_checks.json p_birth_zero_cell_share=0.0887, childless_mass_share_at_p_birth_zero=0.4026.

findings.title: Value function carries sentinel-contaminated finite values (down to -9.997e8) producing 168 in-wealth monotonicity drops; 0.57% of mass sits on nodes with V < -1e6
findings.intended: V monotone nondecreasing in liquid wealth on feasible states; infeasible states excluded from expectations
findings.implemented: -1e10 sentinel enters continuation expectations (interpolation and logsumexp over mixed feasible/infeasible successors), yielding finite but astronomically negative V (min valid -999722548.9, 7612 nodes < -1e6) and drops up to -2.8e7 between adjacent wealth nodes classified 'valid' by the >-1e9 cutoff
findings.economic_consequence: Welfare comparisons and any V-based statistics (entry values, policy counterfactual welfare) are unreliable for low-wealth/parent states carrying 0.57% of mass; monotonicity violations are localized (mass at violation nodes 2.5e-05) so policies/moments are only mildly affected
findings.decisive_test: Histogram V on populated nodes: bimodal with a poisoned tail near -1e6..-1e9; check whether any welfare number in the paper integrates V over the poisoned region
findings.evidence: probe_checks.json V_valid_distribution: min=-999722548.94, p01=-2306718.15, n_below_minus_1e6=7612, populated_mass_below_minus_1e6=0.005677. numeric_checks.json value_monotonicity: 168 pairwise decreases, worst max_drop=-28475336.6 at (ten=1, j=12, n=2, cs=1). locate_invalid_j0.json V_entry_node_parents z2_n1=-766632532.24 (finite, contaminated).

findings.title: diagnostics.py fertility window off by one age vs the KFE (plots j=0..7, KFE applies j=0..6), so the age-46 fertility-policy point is from never-computed zero probabilities
findings.intended: Diagnostic fertility-by-age plot over the operative fertile window

findings.economic_consequence: Display-only: last fertile-age point in fertility_policy_by_age_income_state.png shows 0 expected children spuriously; no mass leakage (age masses exactly uniform, confirming zero rows never enter the reshuffle)
findings.decisive_test: extra_checks.json zero_rowsum_childless_mass_by_age: zeros for j=0..6, 0.01008 for j=7..16

findings.title: Renter-cap bunching (13.2% of renter mass at hR_max=6) keeps the renter cap load-bearing for the rooms moments in the new combined spec
findings.intended: hR_max=6 as a technology cap on rental sizes; rooms moments (prime30_55_childless_renter_mean_rooms, new aggregate rooms target) identified by preferences/prices
findings.implemented: 13.2% of renter service demand sits exactly at the cap (10.7% in the relaxed candidate); the childless-renter mean-rooms moment (model 4.281 vs target 3.805, contribution 1.358) is measured on a censored distribution
findings.economic_consequence: The rooms-block fit and the new aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms target (gap -0.229, contribution 0.314) partly reflect the cap location rather than estimated preferences; consistent with the prior finding that hR_max is a load-bearing lever
findings.decisive_test: Re-solve at the candidate theta with hR_max=7 (tiny grid acceptable) and compare the three renter rooms moments; if they move materially the cap is doing identification work
findings.evidence: numeric_checks.json renter_cap: renter_share_at_cap=0.13209, renter_mass_at_cap=0.04247 of 0.32151; relaxed numeric_checks_light.json renter_share_at_cap=0.10663.

verdict_entries.reasoning: Every factual element of PROV-3 checks out against the live repository. (1) `git ls-files tmp/ code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_recalibration.sh code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_spec_test.sh` returns empty output (exit 0): none of the cited drivers or launch scripts are tracked. (2) `git status --porcelain` shows exactly the untracked entries the finding quotes: '?? tmp/', '?? code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py', '?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_recalibration.sh', '?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_spec_test.sh' (plus two more untracked combined-spec files the finding did not list: '?? code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/tests/test_combined_specification.py' and '?? code/model/tools/compare_intergen_combined_specification.py' — these strengthen, not weaken, the finding). (3) .gitignore line 55 is literally 'output/', and `git check-ignore -v output/model/combined_recalibration/overnight_20260710_report/current_bound_best.json` matches it at .gitignore:55, so the best-candidate artifact is structurally unversionable without a gitignore exception. (4) The files exist and the SHA256 pins are exact matches to the quoted prefixes: run_overnight_combined.py = aa15e99026a6cdc1c54a85b7a8db30b6b06407245f3af8d42b56070c91651376 (173 lines, matching the 'lines 1-173, entire file' citation) and current_bound_best.json = 77651d72e41344acccc8f068695e0062b7e2663f126d291e1f13845a1adf16f5. One precision note on the consequence wording: tmp/ is untracked but NOT gitignored (git check-ignore matched only the output/ file, not tmp/), so a plain `git checkout`/branch switch would not delete it; however `rm -rf tmp` or `git clean -fd` would, and there is no git object storing any of these bytes, so the substance of the fragility claim stands. The torch snapshot fallback is unverifiable (SSH down, per shared context), as the finding already notes. Nothing in the finding mischaracterizes the code or the repository state.
verdict_entries.checked_lines: .gitignore:55: 'output/' (under comment at lines 53-54: '# Large/generated project outputs. Keep scripts and compact summaries in git;'). git status --porcelain output: '?? tmp/', '?? code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py', '?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_recalibration.sh', '?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_spec_test.sh'. git ls-files on all cited paths: empty. git check-ignore -v: '.gitignore:55:output/  output/model/combined_recalibration/overnight_20260710_report/current_bound_best.json'. tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py: exists, 173 lines, sha256 aa15e99026a6cdc1c54a85b7a8db30b6b06407245f3af8d42b56070c91651376. output/model/combined_recalibration/overnight_20260710_report/current_bound_best.json: exists, sha256 77651d72e41344acccc8f068695e0062b7e2663f126d291e1f13845a1adf16f5.
verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR is correct, with the caveat that this is a reproducibility/provenance defect, not a defect in the executed code: it cannot invalidate the numerical result (the lead already reproduced current_bound_best exactly from the local copies), so it must not be escalated to FATAL. It becomes near-FATAL for reproducibility only if the local copies are lost while the torch snapshot remains unreachable; the SHA256 pins recorded now are the correct mitigation.

verdict_entries.checked_lines: Dirty code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/local_panel.py:510 "def run_local_polish("; :521 "income_process: str = \"current\","; :532-536 "normalize_bequest_utility: bool = False, / additional_search_bounds: list[tuple[str, float, float]] | None = None, / additional_targets: dict[str, Any] | None = None (sic, dict[str, float]) / additional_weights ... / fixed_model_overrides: dict[str, Any] | None = None,"; :558-559 "rank_targets.update(additional_targets or {})". HEAD blob via `git show HEAD:code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/local_panel.py` (function at HEAD line 487): signature ends "fixed_theta: dict[str, Any] | None = None, progress: bool = True" with no combined-spec kwargs; body "income = income_process_overrides(income_states)". tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py:135 "summary = lp.run_local_polish("; :145 "income_process=\"rouwenhorst\","; :155-157 "normalize_bequest_utility=True, / additional_search_bounds=[(\"H0\", 1.0, 10.0)], / additional_targets={\"aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85\": ROOMS_TARGET}"; :159,163 "fixed_model_overrides={ ... \"normalize_bequest_utility\": True". git diff HEAD parameters.py/solver.py: "+    P.normalize_bequest_utility = False" and "+    normalize = bool(getattr(P, \"normalize_bequest_utility\", False))"; grep of HEAD parameters.py for normalize_bequest = 0 hits. git status --porcelain: " M" on all five cited files, "??" on tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
The factual core is verified. (1) At HEAD (fe092ca), `run_local_polish` in local_panel.py ends its signature at `progress: bool = True` (HEAD lines 487-507 via `git show`) — none of the five kwargs exist. (2) The dirty tree adds them (local_panel.py:532-536) and the overnight driver passes all five (run_overnight_combined.py: `normalize_bequest_utility=True, additional_search_bounds=[("H0", 1.0, 10.0)], additional_targets=..., additional_weights=..., fixed_model_overrides={...}`). Python raises TypeError on unexpected keyword args, so the proposed decisive test would pass; I did not run `git stash` (mutating git is prohibited for this audit) but the static check is decisive. (3) The diff is not cosmetic: 172 insertions across 5 files including solver.py (+11) and parameters.py (+3) — i.e., the Rouwenhorst income process, bequest normalization, new moment, and extended polish API that define the combined spec exist ONLY as uncommitted diffs. The two new test files are also untracked (`??` in git status).

Two facts sharpen severity beyond the original claim. First, the safety net the finding gestures at is empirically broken: logs/git-backup/launchd.err.log shows the nightly backup failing every run with "Operation not permitted", and `git log --all` confirms zero commits (backup or otherwise) since fe092ca (2026-07-10). So the only durable copies of the load-bearing code are the local dirty working tree and a purgeable torch scratch snapshot that is currently unreachable (SSH down). Second, one detail in the finding is slightly wrong in the right direction: a nightly backup committing the tree would PRESERVE, not orphan, the state — but since the backup is dead, even that weak protection is absent.

On materiality: this does not touch any estimated number. The lead reproduced 14.780020699972585 exactly from the dirty tree, so dirty tree ≡ overnight snapshot on the evaluation path, and the 14.780/13.903 candidates, moments, and policy numbers are all currently regenerable. Hence not FATAL — the estimated model is not invalidated. But MAJOR is the right bucket under this audit's own rubric ("materially changes ... reproducibility"): a headline paper calibration whose evaluation code exists in no commit anywhere, whose drivers TypeError against HEAD, whose tests are untracked, and whose automated backup is silently failing is one `git checkout .`, `stash drop`, or disk failure away from irrecoverable loss of provenance. The fix is one commit of five tracked files plus two untracked tests; until that commit lands, the finding stands at MAJOR.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
Every factual claim in INC-1 checks out against the code and artifacts I read myself.

(1) Provenance of rho_annual: local_panel.py:42 sets `DEFAULT_RHO_Z = 0.85` (the old process's four-year persistence, used at lines 1096-1097 for the "current" process). I computed 0.85**0.25 = 0.9601845894041878, which equals the combined spec's rho_annual with difference exactly 0.0. The overnight driver (tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py:25-26) hard-codes MATCHED_ANNUAL_RHO = 0.9601845894041878 and MATCHED_ANNUAL_INNOVATION_SD = 0.06453733259357768 and passes them with income_process="rouwenhorst" (lines 145-147), so the audited object really used these values.

(2) Variance matching: tracing the implemented Rouwenhorst path (local_panel.py:1045 `rho_period = rho_annual**PERIOD_YEARS`, 1049-1052 `sigma_eps_period = innovation_sd * sqrt(sum rho^(2k))`, `sigma_log = sigma_eps_period/sqrt(1-rho_period^2)`), the stationary log variance is 0.05336713073811972. The old ad hoc grid (local_panel.py:40-41, z=[0.6..1.4], w=[.1,.2,.4,.2,.1]) has weighted log variance 0.0533671307381197; difference 1.4e-17. So the "new" AR(1) is exactly the old grid's persistence and log dispersion re-expressed at annual frequency — no independent empirical source. The snapshot tmp/rouwenhorst_matched_nb120_4h_SOURCE_SNAPSHOT.txt (lines 7-16) states this matching explicitly ("matched to the current draft income process's four-year persistence and unconditional log variance ... 0.0533671307381197").

(3) Magnitude comparison: the repo's own reference constants at local_panel.py:44-45 (ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_RHO=0.90, ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_INNOVATION_SD=0.20, the Sommer-Sullivan-style default) imply stationary log sd 0.4588 through the same code path, vs 0.2310 for the matched process — a factor ~2 in log sd, ~4 in log variance. The claimed 2-3.5x range vs literature (log sd 0.46-0.80) is arithmetically consistent (0.46/0.231≈2.0, 0.80/0.231≈3.5), though the literature values themselves are flagged by the original auditor as from-memory and I did not independently verify them.

(4) Level sd: old grid 0.2191, new normalized 5-point Rouwenhorst grid 0.2323 — the finding's "0.219->0.232, matching was in logs" is exact.

(5) Disclosure: the live draft latex/intergenerational_housing_fertility_paper_draft.tex lines 444-449 and 955-956 disclose the matching ("implying four-year persistence of 0.85 and preserving the stationary log-income dispersion of the benchmark process"). The finding cited "draft lines 444-449, 955-956" under the name paper_draft.tex; the actual filename differs but the line numbers match the real draft file, so this is a filename slip, not a substantive error.

Caveats that do not overturn the finding: (a) the Rouwenhorst discretization code itself (local_panel.py:1038-1076) is a correct implementation of an annual AR(1) time-aggregated to four-year periods and discretized by Rouwenhorst — I traced sigma_eps_period, sigma_log, p=(1+rho_period)/2, the recursive transition build with row renormalization, binomial weights, the ±sigma_log*sqrt(n-1) log grid, and the mean-one normalization; there is no coding defect. The finding is about parameter provenance and magnitude, not a math-to-code mismatch, and it characterizes this correctly ("re-expressed", not "buggy"). (b) The mechanism claims (muted precautionary saving, absorbed by beta/theta0/theta_n/chi) are economically plausible but unquantified until the proposed fixed-theta A/B run is done; the proposed decisive test is the right one.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR is appropriate. Not FATAL: the matching is disclosed in the draft and the snapshot, the implementation is internally consistent and reproducible, and the code contains no defect — so results are not invalidated, but the income-risk primitive lacks an empirical source and sits roughly 2x below the repo's own Sommer-Sullivan reference in stationary log sd, which plausibly contaminates the five wealth/income-conditioned targets and the parameters estimated against them. One nuance the lead should carry: this is a calibration-defensibility finding, not a code-correctness finding; if the audit's FATAL/MAJOR taxonomy is applied strictly to code, the correct statement is "code VERIFIED correct; parameter provenance MAJOR."

verdict_entries.reasoning:
Every code-level claim in INC-2 is accurate; I re-read each cited line myself rather than trusting the quotes.

(1) The dispatcher: income_process_overrides (code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/local_panel.py:1031-1035) has signature `process: str = "current"`, and the "current" branch returns the old grid: DEFAULT_INCOME_GRID_5 = [0.60, 0.80, 1.00, 1.20, 1.40] (local_panel.py:40), DEFAULT_RHO_Z = 0.85 (line 42), with `Pi_z = make_persistent_transition_matrix(z_weights, DEFAULT_RHO_Z)` — the stay/redraw matrix, not Rouwenhorst.

(2) All eight cited call sites invoke it BARE (positional income_states only, no process kwarg), so all default to "current": run_intergen_policy_poc.py:195, audit_intergen_sensitivity_jacobian.py:318, audit_intergen_solver_accuracy.py:208, audit_intergen_mechanism_grid.py:195, audit_intergen_final_best_pathologies.py:148, audit_intergen_parent_credit_margin.py:256, run_intergen_code_repair_comparison.py:74, intergen_surrogate_calibration/validate_proposals.py:42. I additionally grepped each of these eight files for "income_process|income-process|rouwenhorst": none exposes any flag or alternate path to select the Rouwenhorst process. So the finding's "silently" is correct — there is no way to get the matched process through these tools without editing code.

(3) None of the eight tools sets the combined fixed spec. Grep for normalize_bequest_utility / eta_supply / q / delta across all eight files returns only Python loop variables named `delta` in audit_intergen_mechanism_grid.py (perturbation step sizes, not the depreciation parameter). run_intergen_policy_poc.py builds overrides as {base_overrides, income_process_overrides(income_states), theta, case overrides} (lines 193-198) and never imports production_profile_overrides at all.

(4) cli.py: `--income-process ... default="current"` appears three times — panel (line 91), global-de (line 111), local-polish (line 137) — and polish's bare-rouwenhorst defaults are `--income-annual-rho default=0.90` and `--income-innovation-sd default=0.20` (cli.py:138-139), matching the module constants ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_RHO=0.90, ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_INNOVATION_SD=0.20 (local_panel.py:44-45), not the matched 0.9601845894/0.0645373326.

(5) production_profile.py:136-138 ships the old process exactly as quoted ('z_grid': PRODUCTION_Z_GRID.copy(), 'z_weights': PRODUCTION_Z_WEIGHTS.copy(), 'income_shock_persistence': PRODUCTION_INCOME_PERSISTENCE, with the stay/redraw Pi_z at lines 139-141), and validate_production_profile checks only income_states — no check on z_grid, Pi_z, or income_shock_persistence.

(6) The matched constants 0.9601845894041878 / 0.06453733259357768 appear in source in exactly the three files the finding names: run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py:24-25, compare_intergen_bequest_normalization.py:39-40, build_intergen_mechanics_packet.py:418-419. Nowhere else under code/model/.

(7) The merge-order rescue claim is also correct: run_local_panel_case builds overrides = {base_overrides, **(extra_overrides or {}), **income, **theta} (local_panel.py:848-853), so the overnight drivers' explicit income dict overwrote the profile's stale grid — which is exactly why the estimation is fine while the tools are not.

One small imprecision that does not change the finding: the bare-rouwenhorst CLI default sd=0.20 is about 3.1x the matched 0.0645, not "4x" (though combined with the lower rho the implied stationary log-income dispersion gap differs again). The substance — a mismatched, much more dispersed process — stands.

The finding honestly states no contaminated artifact was identified; it is a latent-inconsistency hazard for post-overnight policy/sensitivity/pathology runs on the combined candidate, which would run under rho_z=0.85 stay/redraw on the old grid with old q/delta/eta/bequest spec while appearing legitimate. The proposed decisive test (baseline loss under these tools will not reproduce 14.780) follows directly from the traced dataflow.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR is right, with one clarification for the lead: this is a reproducibility/provenance defect, not an error in the estimated results. The dirty-tree order (which produced and reproduces loss 14.780020699972585) implements the INTENDED object — Rouwenhorst governs. The hazard is that HEAD silently solves a different economy (stale 5-point grid, different weights, structurally different Pi_z; only the persistence scalar coincides at 0.85), AND the run's own metadata.json would still log the Rouwenhorst fingerprint (local_panel.py:625-628 reads from the income dict, not the merged overrides), so the divergence is undetectable from run artifacts. The one-line reorder must be committed (or the spec must stop relying on it) before any reproduction claim from HEAD is trusted.

verdict_entries.reasoning: The finding accurately characterizes the code. Dataflow traced end-to-end: (1) hsv is the raw physical rung size, not a chi-scaled service quantity — solver.py:2051 "hs = P.H_own[ten - 1]" and :2054 "hsrv[i, ten] = hs", passed as hsv at :2166/2175. (2) hbar is in physical units — solver.py:1935 "h_bar[nn, cs] = P.h_bar_0 + P.h_bar_jump + P.h_bar_n * nk". (3) Every owner-surplus site applies chi AFTER netting: kernels.py:915-918 ("ht_c = hsv - owner_h_bar_scale * hbc ... ht_c = owner_service_premium * ht_c"), duplicated at kernels.py:1062-1065, and the non-kernel fallbacks solver.py:2183, 2572, 2669 all read "owner_service_premium * max(hsv - owner_h_bar_scale * SD.hb_flat..., 1e-10)". So the implemented surplus is chi*(H_k - hbar). (4) The paper claims services h = chi_O*H_k for owners (full_draft.tex:521 "\chi_O H_k, & d=O_k,") entering (h - hbar(n,s))^{1-alpha} (full_draft.tex:509), i.e. surplus chi*H_k - hbar. These are algebraically different models unless owner_h_bar_scale = 1/chi, and I verified it is 1.0 in the estimated spec: parameters.py:55 "P.owner_h_bar_scale = 1.0", no override in production_profile.py, and rg finds no owner_h_bar_scale override anywhere in tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/ (the only other setter is a random-draw block in calibration.py:1074 not on this path). (5) The finding's numeric illustration checks out exactly against current_bound_best (output/model/full_audit_20260711/repro/current_nb240.json: theta.chi=1.15, h_bar_0=1.0, h_bar_jump=1.4759, h_bar_n=0.9841): hbar(1 child)=3.4600, so H=4 gives paper surplus 1.15*4-3.46=1.14 vs code 1.15*0.54=0.621. (6) The feasibility claim is also real ON THE ACTUAL GRID, not just hypothetical: for a two-child family at this candidate hbar(2)=4.4441; rung H=4 satisfies chi*H=4.6>4.4441 (paper: feasible, surplus 0.156) but H<hbar so the code floors ht_c at 1e-10 (kernels.py:916-917), which with sigma=2 (oms=-1) yields utility ~ -(tiny)^{-(1-alpha)}, effectively -inf — the code excludes the H=4 rung for two-child owners while the paper form admits it. This changes the minimum feasible owner rung by family size, i.e. the family-size gradient of ownership, exactly as claimed. The renter branch correctly omits chi (solver.py:2150-2158 region uses surplus without owner_service_premium), so the discrepancy is confined to the owner side, matching the finding's scope. Classification as MAJOR (paper-documentation error relative to the estimated code object, or wrong-model estimates if the paper form was intended) is appropriate; the code form chi*(H-hbar) is itself a coherent model, so this is not FATAL to the numerics, but the paper and code do not describe the same utility function and chi's interpretation (service premium on gross services vs multiplier on net surplus) differs between them.

One strengthening beyond the original finding: the feasibility divergence is not merely possible but binds on the actual estimated grid H_own=[2,4,6,8,10] at current_bound_best — two-child families are excluded from the H=4 rung under the code form but would be admitted under the paper form (chi*4=4.60 > hbar(2)=4.444 > 4). The code reading is accurate and the MAJOR severity is warranted; it is neither inflated (not FATAL) nor deflatable to MINOR.

Facts I independently verified (ran a small check with the model venv, no solves):
1. Reverse-engineering confirmed exactly. rho_annual - 0.85**0.25 = 0.0 to machine precision, where 0.85 is DEFAULT_RHO_Z of the old ad hoc stay/redraw process (local_panel.py:42). The matched process's stationary log variance is 0.0533671307381197, identical to the log variance of the old ad hoc grid [0.60,0.80,1.00,1.20,1.40] with weights [0.10,0.20,0.40,0.20,0.10] (local_panel.py:40-41). The snapshot (tmp/rouwenhorst_matched_nb120_4h_SOURCE_SNAPSHOT.txt:7-15) states this matching explicitly ("matched to the current draft income process's four-year persistence and unconditional log variance"). So the "annual AR(1)" primitive is the old ad hoc grid re-expressed; the old grid has no empirical source anywhere in the repo or paper.
2. Magnitude confirmed. Matched stationary log sd = 0.2310 vs the repo's own named reference constants ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_RHO=0.90, ROUWENHORST_ANNUAL_INNOVATION_SD=0.20 (local_panel.py:44-45), which imply stationary log sd 0.4588. Variance ratio = 3.94x. Realized 5-state grids: matched z = {0.613, 0.773, 0.974, 1.227, 1.546} (matches paper line 908 exactly), top/bottom income ratio 2.52; the reference process would give {0.360, ..., 2.255}, top/bottom 6.27. So the cross-sectional income support the model estimates against is compressed ~2.5x in ratio terms relative to the code's own reference, and ~2-3x in sd terms relative to standard persistent-component calibrations (the finder's 2-3.5x range is roughly right; the repo-internal 2.0x alone sustains the point, so the from-memory literature cites are not load-bearing).
3. Disclosure confirmed but circular. Paper lines 444-449: "preserving the stationary log-income dispersion of the benchmark process"; calibration table lines 955-956: "Matched to four-year persistence and stationary log-income dispersion". The benchmark being matched is the uncited ad hoc grid, so the disclosure does not supply an empirical anchor.

Why MAJOR and not MINOR: no income-dispersion parameter is searched, so the risk level is a hard-fixed primitive; all compensation happens through estimated preferences. Of the 15 active moments (calibration.py:146-179), at least five run directly through precautionary saving or income-conditioned wealth: young_childless_renter_liquid_wealth_to_annual_gross_income_2535 (0.179), the old nonhousing wealth-to-income median and the old parent-childless wealth-to-income gap (inherited from the NH_MEDIAN block), own_rate_2534 (down-payment accumulation), and old_age_own_rate. A ~4x understatement of persistent-income variance is first-order for buffer-stock saving in any model of this class, so the estimated beta (which already sits against the external 0.94 annual floor), theta0/theta_n, and chi are estimated against understated risk, and every policy counterfactual that works through down-payment accumulation and constrained households inherits it. The paper's income-risk row is not defensible as stated at the intended venue.

Why not FATAL: this is a disclosed specification choice, not a bug — the Rouwenhorst construction itself is correct (I verified the time-aggregation at local_panel.py:1045-1052: rho_period = rho_annual**4 and sigma_eps_period = sd_annual*sqrt(sum(rho^(2k), k=0..3)) is the exact 4-year-skip aggregation of an annual AR(1), and it preserves the stationary variance). The reproduced 14.780/13.903 losses are internally valid conditional on the process; with 14 free parameters and 15 moments the system is nearly just-identified, so a re-estimation under a defensible process could plausibly restore comparable fit while changing parameter estimates and counterfactuals. The damage is to interpretation, parameter estimates, and policy numbers, not to the arithmetic of the reported results — the definition of MAJOR.

Honest caveat: the exact moment-by-moment sensitivity is unquantified until the proposed fixed-theta A/B (matched vs rho=0.90/sd=0.20 at Nb=120) is run; that remains the right decisive test. But given the 3.94x variance gap in a fixed (non-searched) primitive feeding five targeted wealth/ownership moments, second-order status is implausible.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: Keep MAJOR. Not FATAL: disclosed spec choice with correct discretization math and reproducible losses; a re-estimation under a defensible process could plausibly restore fit (near just-identified), so results are biased-conditional-on-primitive rather than invalid. Not MINOR: 3.94x variance understatement in a fixed, non-searched primitive that feeds >=5 of 15 targeted moments (wealth-to-income and ownership-by-age) and every savings/constraint-mediated counterfactual; income top/bottom support compressed from 6.27 to 2.52. One framing correction for the report: implemented == intended here — the defect is provenance/defensibility of the intended object (a reverse-engineered, empirically unanchored risk level), not a divergence of code from spec.

verdict_entries.reasoning: Every factual element of F2 verifies against the code and artifacts. (1) Bounds: run_overnight_combined.py:156 does pass additional_search_bounds=[("H0", 1.0, 10.0)]. (2) Supply formula: solver.py:5484 is exactly 'supply = P.H0 * (user_cost / P.r_bar) ** P.xi_supply' with user_cost = user_cost_rate * p (solver.py:5483). (3) Parameter dataflow: parameters.py:146 leaves r_bar at the default np.array([0.16]) — the fixed_model_overrides at run_overnight_combined.py:159-164 override q, delta, eta_supply but NOT r_bar, so the old-regime pivot is inherited; parameters.py:332 recomputes user_cost_rate = q + delta + tau_H after overrides, giving 0.0824322 + 0.0432861 + 0.04 = 0.1657115 (tau_H = 0.01*4, parameters.py:82); parameters.py:282-284 maps eta_supply=[1.75] into xi_supply. (4) Arithmetic reproduces exactly from current_repro_exact.json: theta.H0 = 9.999672059746388 (within 3.28e-4 of the 10.0 bound, less than the search min_step 0.0015 — effectively at bound), p_eq = 0.6897625, uc = 0.1143016, (uc/0.16)^1.75 = 0.5551128, supply = 5.5509462, and the rooms moment 5.5511076 differs from this by relative 2.9061e-05 = exactly the stored market residual, proving the equilibrium rooms moment is the supply formula. H0 needed at this price = 5.779970/0.5551128 = 10.412 > 10. Rooms gap -0.228863, loss contribution 6*gap^2 = 0.31427 of 14.780021. (5) Old vs new pivot: old uc_rate = (1.04^4-1)+(1-0.98^4)+0.04 = 0.28749, pivot price r_bar/uc_rate = 0.5565 old vs 0.9655 new — matches the finding's 0.556 vs 0.966 root-cause claim. (6) housing_repro_exact.json: H0 = 8.5596721 (interior), rooms = 5.5849925, gap -0.19498 — matches. In GE the fixed-price "H0 needed = 10.41" understates the shortfall (raising H0 lowers p_eq and deflates the factor further), so unreachability within [1,10] at this candidate is if anything conservative. One nuance, not a refutation: the housing_relaxed arm's interior H0=8.56 shows H0 also trades off against price-sensitive moments, so "rooms cannot discipline H0" is established at the bound-pinned candidate rather than as a global claim; the finding itself already reports the interior housing candidate, so it is internally consistent. The finding's proposed decisive test (re-polish with widened H0 bound or re-pivoted r_bar) is correct and cluster-blocked as stated.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR is the right classification. Not FATAL: the reported candidate is still a genuine strict-converged equilibrium and its loss is correctly computed; what fails is the interpretation as an interior SMM optimum (effective free-parameter and informative-moment counts each drop by one, and per project identification rules the 15-moment/14-parameter margin evaporates). The r_bar=0.16 pivot inherited from the old finance regime is a spec-consistency defect in the combined fixed spec, not a coding bug in the solver: the code does exactly what solver.py:5484 says.

verdict_entries.reasoning: Every load-bearing claim in BQ-1 reproduces under independent code reading. (1) parameters.py:66 defaults the flag to False and solver.py:5611 reads it via getattr with default False, so any solve that does not explicitly override it runs the OLD unnormalized bequest. (2) The estimation used the normalized bequest: tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py:155,163 and run_wave2_lateral.py:74,141,149 explicitly set normalize_bequest_utility=True. (3) I read the override-construction code in all 8 named tools; each builds overrides from base_overrides()/income_process_overrides()/production_profile_overrides() plus theta, and a repo-wide rg for "normalize_bequest_utility" has zero hits in all 8 files, so P.normalize_bequest_utility stays False on any re-solve. The tools' pass-through dicts (extra_overrides in solver_accuracy, case["overrides"] in parent_credit_margin/policy_poc) are populated only with kernel/solve_mode/policy toggles in-file (e.g. solver_accuracy lines 555-564), never with the flag. (4) CLI: only the local-polish subcommand exposes --normalize-bequest-utility (cli.py:159, wired at :266 to run_local_polish, the sole local_panel entry point carrying the parameter, local_panel.py:532/578/621, default False). smoke/solve/diagnostics/local-panel/global-de-panel have no mechanism to set it. (5) Magnitude: normalization subtracts scale*theta1^(1-sigma)/(1-sigma) (solver.py:5615-5620); with sigma=2 this is +scale/theta1, so disabling it drops terminal utility by theta0*(1+theta_n*n)/theta1. With the reproduced candidate theta (theta0=0.1318301350511569, theta_n=0.7679202774006243 from output/model/full_audit_20260711/repro/current_repro_exact.json; theta1=0.01 default, never overridden — rg finds no theta1 override in the overnight driver or production profile) that is 13.18 (n=0), 23.31 (n=1), 33.43 (n=2), matching the finding to rounding. The shift is n-dependent, so fertility-branch value comparisons and hence policies/moments change; the economic-consequence claim is correct in direction and mechanism. The proposed decisive test is valid but unnecessary — the dataflow divergence (True at estimation vs False on replay) is unambiguous from code alone. One reinforcing addition the finding partially states: base_overrides (calibration.py:997-1023) also hard-codes H0=np.array([4.0]) and eta_supply=np.array([1.0]), and income_process_overrides defaults to process="current" (the stale grid), so these tools silently revert the ENTIRE combined fixed spec (q, delta4, eta_supply=1.75, searched H0, Rouwenhorst), not just the bequest normalization — the tools predate the combined spec and cannot reproduce it without explicit extra overrides. Trivial correction: the --normalize-bequest-utility add_argument sits at cli.py:159 in the current dirty tree, not 158 (the neighboring quote at :158 is --fixed-chi); this does not affect the finding. Nothing in BQ-1 mischaracterizes the code.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR is correct; do not escalate to FATAL. The estimated candidate itself is clean (overnight drivers set the flag True and the lead reproduced the loss exactly), so the defect contaminates only post-estimation re-solves. One framing refinement: the bequest flag is not the only silently dropped element — the same 8 tools also revert q, delta4, eta_supply=1.75, searched H0, and the Rouwenhorst income process to legacy defaults (base_overrides hard-codes H0=[4.0], eta_supply=[1.0]; income_process_overrides defaults to the stale "current" process). The finding should be read as "the intergen tool layer predates the combined spec and cannot replay it," with the bequest flag the most policy-relevant instance; any decisive test should compare the full combined override dict, not the flag in isolation.

verdict_entries.reasoning: Every element of the finding checks out against direct reads. (1) current_bound_best_parameters.csv line 2 shows H0=9.999672059746388 in [1,10] with near_bound_2pct=True — 0.0036% of the range below the cap, effectively at the bound even though the file's exact-equality at_bound flag is False. (2) current_bound_best_fit.csv line 2 shows the identifying moment aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85 at 5.5511 vs target 5.7800 (gap -0.2289, weight 6). (3) run_overnight_combined.py:156-158 confirms H0 was added as a searched 14th parameter with the rooms target at weight 6, and local_panel.py:533-581 confirms the plumbing (extra_bounds/rank_targets.update), so H0 was genuinely searched. (4) The direction is right: H0 is the supply scale (solver.py:1647 Hs=H0*(r/r_bar)^xi_supply; solver.py:1025 p_target=r_bar*(hd/H0)^(1/xi)/user_cost_rate), so higher H0 lowers prices and raises the rooms moment; an undershooting rooms moment with H0 pinned at the top is consistent with a bound-constrained, not interior, optimum. (5) Bound count accurate: c_bar_0 (upper 1.28), chi (upper 1.15), h_bar_0 (lower 1.0), tenure_choice_kappa (lower 0.0) all carry at_bound=True, plus H0 near-bound — 4-5 of 14 as claimed. Caveats: the phrase "at its upper bound" slightly overstates the CSV's own at_bound=False flag (immaterial at 0.0036% of range under a pattern search with min_step 0.0015); some other bounds may be deliberate external restrictions, which affects framing of the effective-parameter-count point but not the factual H0 claim; strict proof of bound-constraint requires the outward gradient, so the proposed [1,20] re-polish remains the decisive test. Per CLAUDE.md, at-bound estimates must be reported as such, so MAJOR severity is appropriate.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: MAJOR stands. Not FATAL: the loss and fit are still valid at the constrained optimum, and the CSV pipeline itself flags near_bound_2pct=True, so the information is not hidden — but any interpretation of H0 as data-identified supply scale, the just-identified 15/14 counting, and any policy exercise using H0≈10 inherit the bound. If several of the other binding bounds (chi, c_bar_0) are documented external restrictions, the "effective interior problem is 9-10 parameters" framing softens for those, but the H0-specific claim is unaffected because H0's [1,10] range was a search bound introduced in this spec, not an externally justified restriction.

verdict_entries.reasoning: The finding accurately characterizes the code. (1) weighted_quantile returns an uninterpolated order statistic: utils.py:228-231 does searchsorted on cumulative weights and returns q[idx] = values[pos], so the weighted median is always an element of the input value set. (2) old_nonhousing_wealth_to_income_median_6575 is a hard target in the active target set candidate_replacement_post_audit_v1: it enters at calibration.py:106 (target 2.23046078) in NH_MEDIAN_V1, survives the exclusion filters into ROOMGAP_14MOMENT_V1 (calibration.py:146-161) and POST_AUDIT_V1 (calibration.py:170-179, which removes only housing_increment_1to2), with weight 0.8 flowing the same inheritance path (calibration.py:429-430 -> 476-491 -> 494-503). (3) The underlying ratio values are exactly b-grid nodes divided by one constant: solver.py:4914 computes nonhousing_ratio = bg[positive]/yj; the 65-75 window is age indices 12-14 (age_to_index at solver.py:4614-4616 with period_years=4, age_start=18, J=17 from parameters.py:27-31), all >= J_R=12 (parameters.py:32), so income_at_state (solver.py:127-131) returns P.income[i,j] which parameters.py:417-420 sets to the scalar P.pension for every income state/location i and every retired age. The pension (parameters.py:408-436) depends only on tau_pay, J_R, J, and the fixed worker income profile — none of the 14 searched parameters — and the b-grid is fixed (b_max=30, power=1.5; production_profile.py:29,36,143,150). Hence the moment takes values only in a fixed ~Nb-point set (120 at search, 240 at verify) and is piecewise-constant in all searched directions, jumping only when the 0.5 cumulative-weight point crosses a grid node. This matches the finding's intended-vs-implemented characterization and the MAJOR classification on search-smoothness/identification grounds. The proposed decisive test (perturb beta_annual, observe step function) would show exactly this, but the mathematical structure already makes it certain, so I did not burn a solve on the running battery.

P.income[i, j] = P.pension"; parameters.py:424-431 "return P.tau_pay * 1.0 * (P.J_R / max(P.J - P.J_R, 1))"; calibration.py:104-106 "old_nonhousing_wealth_to_income_median_6575\": 2.23046078"; calibration.py:146-161 (ROOMGAP_14MOMENT_V1 inherits NH_MEDIAN_V1, median not excluded); calibration.py:170-179 (POST_AUDIT_V1 removes only housing_increment_1to2); calibration.py:429-430 "old_nonhousing_wealth_to_income_median_6575\": 0.8"; calibration.py:645-649 (TARGET_SETS registry entry for candidate_replacement_post_audit_v1); production_profile.py:29,36 "PRODUCTION_B_MAX = 30.0 / PRODUCTION_B_GRID_POWER = 1.5"
verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: Keep MAJOR, but with one correction to the consequence text: the claim that the reported miss (model ~1.03/0.38 vs target 2.23) is "partly an artifact of step response" is overstated. Quantization error is bounded by one b-grid step in ratio units, which is far smaller than the ~1.2 miss, so the poor fit is real, not a discretization artifact. The MAJOR classification rests correctly on (a) the piecewise-constant objective in beta/theta0/theta_n directions degrading pattern/Nelder-Mead search and possibly stranding candidates on plateaus, and (b) violation of the standing project rule "Never hard-target median moments (grid-discrete)" plus the June 22 surrogate-calibration finding that discrete median moments break smooth outer-loop search.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
Factual content of F2 is fully confirmed; the claimed MAJOR severity is inflated for fit and policy, and I recommend downgrading the economic-materiality component to MINOR while keeping a mandatory reporting caveat.

CONFIRMED facts (independently recomputed):
1. H0 is at the bound in current_bound_best: theta.H0 = 9.999672059746388 vs upper bound 10.0 (run_overnight_combined.py:156). Distance 3.3e-5 relative — binding for any practical purpose.
2. Arithmetic verified end-to-end: user_cost_rate = q + delta + tau_H = 0.0824322 + 0.0432786 + 0.04 = 0.165711 (parameters.py:154, tau_H = 0.01*4 at :82); at p_eq = 0.689762 this gives uc = 0.114303, supply factor (0.114303/0.16)^1.75 = 0.555119, and 9.999672 * 0.555119 = 5.55101, matching the recorded rooms moment 5.551108 to within the 2.9e-5 market residual. H0 needed at that price = 5.779970/0.555119 = 10.412 > 10. Equilibrium adjustment makes it worse (higher H0 lowers p, lowers uc, shrinks the factor), so at THIS theta the target is indeed unreachable in [1,10].
3. Root cause verified: r_bar = 0.16 hard-coded (parameters.py:146 "P.r_bar = np.array([0.16])") is a pivot from the old finance regime; the new q/delta cut user_cost_rate from ~0.29 to 0.1657, deflating the supply factor at any price, and the [1,10] H0 bound was not rescaled by the overnight spec.

WHY THE SEVERITY IS INFLATED:
(a) Loss materiality is ~2%. Rooms contributes 0.3143 of 14.780 (2.1%) in current_bound_best and 0.2281 of 13.903 (1.6%) in the housing candidate. Even full elimination of the rooms gap would move the headline loss by at most 2%, and full elimination is not attainable because closing it worsens the ownership moments that are already overshooting (own_rate gap +0.078 at weight 100, contribution 0.605; old_age_own_rate gap +0.184 at weight 160, contribution 5.391 — the single largest term). Raising H0 lowers p_eq and pushes ownership further above target, so the unconstrained improvement is a fraction of 0.314.
(b) The BETTER candidate is not bound-constrained. housing_repro_exact.json has H0 = 8.559672 (interior), p_eq = 0.756464, loss 13.903. At its price the H0 needed to hit the rooms target is 8.859 < 10 — the target was REACHABLE within [1,10] and the optimizer declined, settling at rooms gap -0.195. This is revealed-preference evidence that a rooms undershoot of ~-0.2 is a genuine demand-side tradeoff against the ownership overshoots, not a bound artifact, and that the shadow value of the H0<=10 constraint at the 14.780 point is small (plausibly <0.1-0.2 loss units). F2's headline "rooms target unreachable within [1,10]" is theta-conditional and false for the 13.903 candidate. (Caveat: the two candidates differ in 8 theta components, not H0 alone — H0 differs by exactly 1.44 with an identical decimal tail, indicating shared seed lineage and coarse coordinate steps — so this is bounding evidence, not a clean one-parameter experiment.)
(c) The identification claim is overstated. Losing one effective parameter at a bound leaves 15 moments against 13 effective free parameters — MORE overidentified, not less; there is no underidentification event. The rooms moment still carries information (it is what pushes H0 to the bound). What is actually lost is interiority: standard interior-SMM inference for H0 does not apply at the 14.780 point, and the point is a KKT/bound-constrained optimum. That is a reporting and inference caveat, not an invalidated estimate.
(d) Reproducibility and policy are untouched. The lead reproduced the candidate exactly (loss_gap 0.0, residual 2.9e-5); policy counterfactuals run from the reproduced equilibrium allocation. Moving H0 from 10.0 to ~10.4-10.5 (+4-5% supply shift at fixed price) would change equilibrium moments by second-order amounts; the cross-candidate comparison shows +16.8% H0 coexisting with essentially the same rooms level (5.585 vs 5.551).

WHAT REMAINS GENUINELY ACTIONABLE: (i) Project SMM rules require every reported parameter to be flagged when near a bound — H0 at 9.9997/10 in current_bound_best MUST be disclosed wherever that candidate is reported, and the point must be described as bound-constrained; (ii) the stale r_bar = 0.16 pivot is a spec-hygiene defect for the NEXT search round (widen the H0 bound or re-pivot r_bar to the new user_cost_rate — the two are exactly interchangeable since H0 and r_bar enter only through H0*(uc/r_bar)^1.75); (iii) the proposed decisive test (re-polish with widened bound) is reasonable but cluster-side/blocked, and my expectation from (b) is that it lowers the 14.780 loss by well under 0.3 and does not overturn the 13.903 candidate's ranking.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: Downgrade MAJOR -> MINOR on economic materiality, with a mandatory reporting caveat retained. Grounds: (1) rooms misfit is 2.1% of the 14.780 loss and 1.6% of the 13.903 loss; (2) the better (13.903) candidate is interior in H0 with the target reachable (required H0=8.86 < 10) yet a near-identical rooms gap, so the ~-0.2 undershoot is a demand-side tradeoff against overshooting ownership moments, not a bound artifact, and the constraint's shadow value at the 14.780 point is small; (3) no underidentification occurs (15 moments vs 13 effective params at the bound); (4) reproducibility and the candidate ranking are unaffected. Keep MAJOR only in the narrow sense that reporting the 14.780 candidate WITHOUT flagging H0 at its bound would violate the project's own SMM reporting rules — the required fix is disclosure plus re-pivoting r_bar (or widening the H0 bound) in the next search round, not invalidation of current results.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
The code/file reading in PROV-3 is accurate and the MAJOR severity is warranted — but strictly as a reproducibility/provenance risk, not as anything that touches the estimated model or the reported numbers.

What I verified directly:
1. Untracked status confirmed. `git status --porcelain` shows `?? tmp/`, `?? code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py`, `?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_recalibration.sh`, `?? code/cluster/submit_intergen_combined_spec_test.sh`, `?? code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/tests/test_combined_specification.py`. `git ls-files tmp/ code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py` returns empty (the proposed decisive test passes). `.gitignore` contains `output/`, so all result artifacts (current_bound_best.json, housing_relaxed_best.json, the audit folder itself) are structurally unversionable at their current paths.
2. The economically substantive combined-spec constants exist in exactly TWO local files, both untracked. Grepping the full-precision spec constants `0.9601845894041878` (rouwenhorst rho_annual) and `5.779970481941968` (new aggregate rooms target) across tmp/, code/model/, CALIBRATION_STATUS.md, and docs/model/ hits only `tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py` and `code/model/tools/run_intergen_combined_recalibration.py`. Neither constant appears in any tracked file, including the tracked `code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/production_profile.py`. So the repo at HEAD cannot re-derive the 14.780 loss: the fixed spec (q, delta, eta_supply, income process, H0 bound, 15th target) is not in version control anywhere.
3. AGGRAVATOR the original finding missed: the nightly launchd git-backup safety net is dead. `logs/git-backup/launchd.err.log` contains 52 identical lines `/bin/bash: .../run_daily_git_backup.sh: Operation not permitted` (one per nightly attempt, ~52 consecutive failures, last at Jul 10 23:40), the only success log is dated May 11, and `git log --grep=backup` shows only the setup commits — no automated backup commit has ever landed. The backup script (run_daily_git_backup.sh:43-53) stages `code`, `docs`, `latex`, `ops` — it would have captured the code/ copy of the spec had it worked, and it explicitly never stages `tmp/`. So the "a defense exists via the nightly backup" rebuttal fails on the evidence.
4. The candidate JSON is partially self-documenting but not spec-complete. current_bound_best.json carries theta (all 14 estimates at full precision), the full fit table, and bounds — but its `path` points to `/scratch/td2248/projects/Fertility_Spring26_20260710_combined_spec/...` (scratch filesystems are purge-prone) and the JSON does NOT embed the fixed spec (q, delta, eta_supply, income process, target definitions). Recovery of the headline number therefore requires theta (in ignored output/) PLUS the spec (in untracked files) PLUS tracked model code.

Why this is MAJOR and not more or less:
- Not FATAL: no reported number is wrong. The lead already reproduced 14.780020699972585 exactly (gap 0.0) from tracked model code plus the spec transcribed from the untracked driver, so as of today the result is verifiable. Zero moments move; interpretation, fit, and policy conclusions are unchanged.
- Not MINOR: the failure mode is a total, unrecoverable loss of the headline calibration's defining spec from a single mundane event (`rm -rf tmp` plus a scratch purge, or a clean checkout), and the two mitigations one would normally cite — the nightly backup and the cluster snapshot — are respectively broken (52 straight failures) and currently unreachable. For a project whose CLAUDE.md makes the backup routine a stated guarantee, "reproducibility currently depends on files no automated or manual process protects" materially changes the reproducibility assessment, which is exactly the MAJOR criterion.
- The fix is trivial (commit the four untracked code/ files and archive the two tmp/ drivers plus the three report JSONs, ~200KB total; separately repair the launchd TCC permission), which bounds the practical cost but does not lower the current severity — severity is assessed on the audited state, and in the audited state the exposure is real.

verdict_entries.reasoning: Every factual element of T-DATA-1 verified independently. (1) The builder edit is uncommitted: `git diff HEAD -- code/data/mms_center_periphery/build_intergen_one_market_housing_targets.R` shows exactly two additions — `+    age <= 85,` and the six-line `post_moment(rows, "aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85", weighted_mean_safe(df$rooms, df$hhwt), ...)` block — and `git show HEAD:<builder> | grep aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms\|age <= 85` returns nothing (exit 1), so at HEAD the builder neither applies the age<=85 cap nor emits this moment. (2) The model-side mapping is also uncommitted: `git show HEAD:code/model/intergen_housing_fertility/calibration.py | grep aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms` is empty, while the working tree defines it in extract_moments at calibration.py:1090-1092 as aggregate_housing_demand / max(total_mass, 1e-12); git status marks calibration.py `M`. (3) The candidate-only flag is real and comes from the builder's own hard-coded status string (builder line 177: `status <- "candidate; re-audit source/sample/formula before SMM use"`) stamped onto every row, plus README text at builder line 254: "These outputs are candidate replacement moments, not approved SMM targets." (4) The output CSV row matches byte-exact: row 2 of output_intergen_one_market_targets/intergen_one_market_acs_housing_targets.csv is `aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85,5.779970481941968,...,candidate; re-audit source/sample/formula before SMM use`, identical to the digit to `ROOMS_TARGET = 5.779970481941968` at tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py:27. CSV mtime is Jul 10 19:50, consistent with the claimed regeneration ~3h before the overnight. One point where reality is STRONGER than the finding: the CSV is not merely regenerated-but-dirty — it is entirely untracked (`git ls-files --error-unmatch` fails), so HEAD contains no version of the target artifact at all, not even a stale one. Minor nit only: the value column is the 2nd CSV field, and the calibration.py mapping strictly spans lines 1090-1092 (line 1088 is the total_mass fetch feeding it); neither affects the substance. The characterization is accurate: the 15th target's data-side construction, artifact, and model-side moment mapping all exist only in the dirty tree, and the target is self-labeled as unapproved for SMM use. MAJOR (reproducibility/provenance) is the right class — the number itself is consistent end-to-end, so it is not FATAL.

Not FATAL: the target value is internally consistent (builder -> CSV -> driver byte-exact) and the lead already reproduced the candidate loss, so no estimated number is wrong. But the finding slightly understates the reproducibility gap: the CSV artifact is fully untracked (no HEAD version exists), and the proposed decisive test (git stash + rerun builder) would also require the IPUMS extract .dta locally; a cheaper decisive check is the two `git show HEAD | grep` calls plus `git ls-files --error-unmatch` on the CSV, all of which I ran and which confirm HEAD cannot produce the 15th target or its model mapping.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
The factual claims all check out and, more importantly, the proposed decisive test EXECUTES AND FLIPS: I recomputed both candidates' objectives from the saved audit fit tables (output/model/full_audit_20260711/repro/current_repro_exact.json and housing_repro_exact.json) using W=diag(1/boot_se^2) from moment_se.csv.

Quantified results:
(1) Weight-ratio claims verified exactly. used_w/(1/SE^2): own_family_gap 45/23650=0.0019, old_age_own_rate 160/6313=0.0253, old nh median 0.8/127.3=0.0063, old nh gap 2.0/2.2=0.930. Max/min spread of the ratio across the 12 SE-covered moments = 488.8 (~500x as claimed). tfr, childless_rate, and aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85 have no SE (the first two are flagged "source-unconfirmed" in the CSV itself).
(2) Ranking flip CONFIRMED. Used weights (all 15 moments): current=14.780, housing=13.937 (housing better by 5.7%). Inverse-variance on the 12 SE moments: current=702.0, housing=849.9 (current better by 17%). Renormalized to the same total weight: 8.25 vs 9.99. Keeping the 3 no-SE moments at their used weights: 8.66 vs 10.90. The flip is robust to normalization and to how the missing-SE moments are handled. Driver: old_nonhousing_wealth_to_income_median_6575 (used weight 0.8, IV weight 127) — housing candidate's model value 0.379 vs current's 1.029 against target 2.230, contributing squared-t 436 vs 184; partially offset by own_rate_2534 where housing nails the target (t=0.31 vs current's t=-4.98).
(3) So the overnight's cross-candidate ordering — the basis for treating housing_relaxed_best (13.9) as the better point — is entirely an artifact of the ad hoc W. If that candidate were adopted as canonical, every downstream policy number inherits a weight-dependent selection.

Severity assessment: MAJOR is the right classification, not inflated, for the interpretation/ranking channel. But two caveats bound it away from FATAL and should temper the prescription:
(a) SMM with any fixed positive-definite W is consistent; the point estimates are not invalidated. The paper (per shared context) reports calibration losses, not SEs or J-tests, so "no defensible SEs" is a limitation of what can be claimed, not an error in what was claimed.
(b) Inverse-variance weighting is NOT the obviously correct benchmark here. Under IV the model is overwhelmingly rejected at both candidates (squared t-stats of 213/250 on old_age_own_rate, 184/436 on old nh median wealth; totals 702/850 with 15 moments and 14 free parameters, i.e., ~1 overidentifying restriction). With misspecification of this magnitude the standard advice is precisely NOT to use the efficient W, because it loads the estimator onto sampling precision (ACS n=1.8M) rather than economically salient moments. The boot SEs also exclude simulation error and any misspecification component. So the correct conclusion is "candidate ranking and loss statements are weighting-sensitive and must be reported as such / the D10 program was left unfinished," not "the IV-weighted objective is the true one."

Net: CONFIRMED at MAJOR. The single strongest fact supporting MAJOR is the reproducible ranking flip between the two headline candidates; the finding's own framing ("consistent but efficiency-arbitrary," no defensible inference from this loss) is accurate and not overstated.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: Uphold MAJOR, with scope narrowed: the binding consequence is that the cross-candidate ranking (housing_relaxed_best 13.94 vs current_bound_best 14.78) flips under inverse-variance weights (849.9 vs 702.0; flip robust to normalization and to handling of the 3 no-SE moments), so any adoption of the "better" candidate and all loss-based comparisons are weighting-arbitrary; and no SEs/J-tests can be built on this loss. It is NOT FATAL: point estimation under an arbitrary fixed W remains consistent, and pure IV weighting would itself be inadvisable given both candidates show |t|>13 misses on two moments (badly misspecified regime), so the remedy is reporting weighting sensitivity and completing the D10 weighting program, not declaring the IV-weighted objective correct. A secondary aggravator worth carrying to the lead: the two headline demographic targets (tfr, childless_rate) have no SE because their data source is "source-unconfirmed" per the SE file itself.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
Every factual assertion in T3 checks out against the code and the run artifacts I read directly; the finding does not mischaracterize the implemented objects.

(1) Target and weight: calibration.py:96 sets the gap target 1.00744952 and calibration.py:420 sets its weight 2.0 in the base CANDIDATE_REPLACEMENT_V1 dicts; the active set candidate_replacement_post_audit_v1 (calibration.py:170-179 targets, 494-503 weights, registered at 645-648) inherits both unchanged via dict spreading from the ROOMGAP_14MOMENT chain — and the run artifacts confirm it empirically: both fit CSVs carry the gap row with target 1.00744952 and weight 2.0.

(2) Moment implementation: solver.py:4961 computes exactly the intended object (parent minus childless old nonhousing wealth-to-income ratio), consistent with the cited range.

(3) theta_n's entry point: theta_n appears in the solver only at solver.py:5610 inside bequest_utility_vec, `scale = P.theta0 * max(1 + P.theta_n * nk, 0)` — it is purely a parent-tilt on the bequest motive. Among the 15 targeted moments, the gap moment is the only one that conditions old wealth on parent status; theta_n's other channels (into tfr/childless_rate/own_family_gap via the value of children) are exactly the channels the June 18 Jacobian found near-collinear with psi_child/kappa_fert.

(4) Numeric claims recomputed from the artifacts: current_bound_best gap contribution 0.1190507/14.780021 = 0.805% and model value 1.2514277 (current_bound_best_fit.csv); housing_relaxed_best 0.1150453/13.903465 = 0.827% and model value 1.2472883 (housing_relaxed_best_fit.csv). theta_n = 0.7679202774006243 vs 1.109009045400624 (44.4% apart) with theta0 = 0.1318301350511569 bit-identical in both parameter CSVs — the identical-to-16-digits theta0 across two independently polished arms is itself additional evidence the theta0/theta_n direction is flat in the objective.

(5) The June 18 Jacobian audit exists and says what is quoted: docs/model/intergen_sensitivity_jacobian_audit_20260618.md lines 126-127 give cos(psi_child,theta_n)=0.975 and cos(kappa_fert,theta_n)=-0.946, and lines 77-78,85 state the theta0/theta_n column norms are tiny and that fresh gap-type moments are needed "before treating theta0 and theta_n as internally calibrated." The finding correctly discloses this predates the current spec/target system.

Caveats (do not overturn the verdict): (a) the two candidates come from different bound arms — housing_relaxed_best has wider bounds (chi ub 1.6 vs 1.15, h_bar_0 lb 0.25, h_bar_jump ub 3.5, h_bar_n ub 3.0) and differs in H0, alpha_cons, chi, h_bar_0, h_bar_jump, h_bar_n, kappa_fert, psi_child as well — so the theta_n contrast is confounded by the arm difference and is evidence of, not proof of, set-identification; the proposed fix-theta_n-and-repolish test is genuinely the decisive one and has not been run. (b) "near-equal-loss" is a mild overstatement (14.78 vs 13.90, 6% apart), and per the wave-1 design the relaxed arm is diagnostic-only, though both arms use the identical target system so the loss comparison is meaningful. (c) "only moment that separates theta_n" is a local-Jacobian statement, not a literal exclusion restriction — theta_n has indirect fertility-moment channels — but those channels are the documented near-collinear ones. This is an identification/design finding rather than a code bug; MAJOR is the right class, not FATAL.

It is not a code defect (the gap moment and bequest scale are implemented as intended), so FATAL is inappropriate; but a ~0.8%-of-loss single moment carrying the only direct discipline on theta_n, plus documented near-collinearity and a bit-identical theta0 across arms, materially undermines any bequest-tilt counterfactual. verdict_entries.reasoning:
The code reading is correct and the severity is MAJOR — if anything the finding understates one implication. Decisive no-solve test (output/model/full_audit_20260711/scripts/verify_T1_median_discreteness.py, results in target_audit/verify_T1_median_discreteness.json): I rebuilt P through the exact overnight override path (base_overrides + production profile + combined fixed spec + Rouwenhorst income + candidate theta) and found (1) the annual pension in the 65-75 window is the single value 0.4296 across all 5 income states and all three window ages (66, 70, 74; all retired since J_R=12 and the window starts at j=12), and (2) BOTH reproduced candidate medians are float-exact b-grid nodes divided by that pension: current_bound_best median 1.028539257719458 = bg[49]/0.4296 (abs diff 4.4e-16), housing_first_best median 0.37893551600190634 = bg[47]/0.4296 (abs diff 1.7e-16). The moment's codomain is at most 120 fixed values (grid depends only on Nb/b_max/b_frac_*/b_grid_power and pension only on tau_pay and the fixed income profile — none searched, confirmed by the identical 0.4296 across the two different thetas), so the moment is exactly piecewise constant in all 14 searched parameters.

Materiality numbers: weight 0.8, target 2.23046078. Loss contributions from the lead's exact reproductions: 1.1557/14.7800 = 7.8% (current) and 2.7425/13.9371 = 19.7% (housing) — the finding's 8-20% claim is exact. The local ratio-grid spacing near the candidate medians is 0.325-0.379, i.e. 15-17% of the target per node. A single one-node jump of the median changes the total objective by 0.54-0.71 loss units at current_bound_best and 0.88-1.24 at housing_first_best. That is comparable to or LARGER than the entire 0.84 loss gap between the two headline candidates (14.780 vs 13.937), so the candidate ranking at the headline level is within one grid-step of this single moment. Moreover the two candidates' medians differ by exactly two grid nodes, so the 1.59 cross-candidate difference in this moment's loss contribution is literally a two-node quantization artifact, not a smooth economic difference.

Why MAJOR and not FATAL: (a) the direction of the miss is real economics, not an artifact — grid nodes near 2.23 exist (spacing ~0.32, best achievable gap ~0.16, min contribution ~0.02), so the model could in principle hit the target on this grid; both candidates sit 3-6 node-steps below it, meaning old nonhousing wealth is genuinely far too low; (b) it is one of 15 moments with modest weight 0.8, and wave-1 DE is population-based and reasonably robust to piecewise-constant objectives; the damage concentrates in wave-2 lateral/local polish and in fine ranking. But interpretation of fit differences below ~1 loss unit, the wave-2 polish geometry (plateaus in beta_annual/theta0/theta_n directions), and the reported per-moment miss magnitudes are all materially affected, and the moment violates the standing external restriction 'Never hard-target median moments (grid-discrete)' plus the June 22 surrogate finding. The corroborating detail that calibration.py's own issue registry marks this moment 'needs-fix' (lines 273-278, for a separate annual-vs-period denominator concern) further undercuts treating it as a clean hard target. Severity MAJOR stands.

verdict_entries.severity_adjustment: Keep MAJOR. Quantified support: one grid-node move of this single moment shifts the total objective by 0.54-1.24 loss units, exceeding the 0.84 gap between the two reported candidates, and the entire cross-candidate difference in this moment (1.59 loss units) is a two-node quantization artifact. Not FATAL because the miss direction is genuine (nodes near the 2.23 target exist but both candidates sit 3-6 steps below), the moment carries only weight 0.8 of a 15-moment objective, and the wave-1 DE search tolerates step objectives; the contamination is confined to fine candidate ranking, wave-2 local polish geometry, and interpretation of this moment's miss magnitude.

verdict_entries.reasoning:
All three factual legs of T-DATA-1 check out, but their evidentiary weight is uneven, and the economically material core is narrower than the write-up suggests.

VERIFIED FACTS. (1) The builder edit is real and uncommitted: `git diff HEAD` on build_intergen_one_market_housing_targets.R shows exactly `+    age <= 85,` (working-tree line 145) and the new `post_moment(rows, "aggregate_mean_occupied_rooms_18_85", ...)` block (lines 179-184). (2) Worse than "uncommitted edit": the output CSV directory `code/data/mms_center_periphery/output_intergen_one_market_targets/` is entirely UNTRACKED (`git ls-files` returns nothing; no git log), so at HEAD neither the builder logic nor the data artifact for this target exists in version control. (3) The model-side mapping is a +4-line uncommitted diff in calibration.py (lines 1088-1092). (4) The driver hard-codes `ROOMS_TARGET = 5.779970481941968` (tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/run_overnight_combined.py:27), and tmp/overnight_combined_20260710/ is itself untracked (`??` in git status) — so the CSV is provenance documentation, not on the evaluation path.

MATERIALITY QUANTIFICATION. Direct fit impact is second-order. From the lead's exact loss recomputation (target_audit_recompute_output.txt): at current_bound_best the moment contributes 0.314269 of 14.780021 (2.13%, gap -0.229 rooms on a 5.78 target, ~4% relative miss); at housing_relaxed_best it contributes 0.227891 of 13.903465 (1.64%, gap -0.195). Deleting the moment mechanically moves the headline losses by ~0.2-0.3 — small next to old_age_own_rate (36-45% of loss). So the finding does NOT materially change the reported fit.

WHAT KEEPS IT MAJOR ANYWAY. The target's function is not its loss share; it is the sole new moment introduced to identify the newly freed parameter H0 (search bound [1,10] in the combined fixed spec). Without it the system is 14 parameters / 14 moments and H0 is identified only through GE spillovers into the other rooms moments — i.e., the identification claim of the estimated 14-parameter specification rests on this target. Under the repo's own SMM discipline (any target addition must name the parameter it identifies and be a reviewed target system), an unreviewed target that anchors a new free parameter is an interpretation-level issue, not a cosmetic one. The reproducibility clause of the MAJOR definition is also literally met (HEAD cannot regenerate the target system), though this is largely duplicative of the tree-wide provenance finding (PROVENANCE_MANIFEST.md already establishes that at HEAD the drivers crash with TypeError, so HEAD-irreproducibility is a property of the entire overnight, not specific to this target).

WHERE THE FINDING OVERSTATES. The "flagged candidate-only by its own artifact" leg carries almost no incremental signal: the status string `candidate; re-audit source/sample/formula before SMM use` is set ONCE as boilerplate for every row (builder line 177, `status <- "candidate; re-audit source/sample/formula before SMM use"`), and the same flag sits on rows whose values are already hard-coded into the production target set — e.g. CSV `prime30_55_childless_renter_mean_rooms,3.805288099342435,...,candidate; re-audit...` matches calibration.py:97 `"prime30_55_childless_renter_mean_rooms": 3.80528810,`, and likewise owner_share_rooms_ge6 = 0.59613112 (calibration.py:100) and owner_minus_renter = 2.41876173 (calibration.py:142). So the flag does not distinguish the new target from the long-approved production moments derived from the same builder. Also, "unapproved" is an inference from the tree; the repo cannot show whether the user signed off on the target (the lead/user context must adjudicate approval, not the file system).

BOTTOM LINE. MAJOR is defensible, but on narrower grounds than claimed: (a) it is the identifying moment for the new free parameter H0, so the estimated specification's identification story depends on an unreviewed, version-control-invisible target; (b) it is one concrete instance of the tree-wide HEAD-irreproducibility already ranked by provenance. If the lead consolidates reproducibility into the provenance finding, the standalone residual here (unreviewed target anchoring H0; value itself verified byte-exact and sample definition plausible) is borderline MAJOR/MINOR — I would keep MAJOR conditional on the H0-identification role, and explicitly not on the loss impact (2%) or the boilerplate "candidate" flag. Decisive test as proposed (rerun builder at HEAD, grep for the moment) is correct but should note the CSV is untracked, so a stash alone will not remove it from disk; checking `git ls-files` on the output dir is the cleaner one-liner. Cluster-side confirmation that the snapshot carried the same diffs is blocked (SSH unavailable), as pre-agreed.
